scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Ich bin}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Ich bin | Meaning: I am
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en German? Ich bin

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Du bist}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Du bist | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en German? Du bist

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'He/She/It is'? {{c1::Er/Sie/Es ist}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Er/Sie/Es ist | Meaning: He/She/It is
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She/It is' en German? Er/Sie/Es ist

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Wir sind}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Wir sind | Meaning: We are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en German? Wir sind

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Ihr seid}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Ihr seid | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en German? Ihr seid

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'You are (formal) / They are'? {{c1::Sie sind}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Sie sind | Meaning: You are (formal) / They are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are (formal) / They are' en German? Sie sind

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::sein}}
explanation: German verb conjugation of 'sein' is highly irregular and one of the most basic elements of A1 grammar.
usage: Term: sein | Example: glücklich sein (to be happy)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en alemán? sein

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To negate the verb 'sein' in German, you place the negation particle {{c1::nicht}} after the verb. Example: I am not ready = {{c2::Ich bin nicht bereit}}.
explanation: 'nicht' is used to negate adjectives, adverbs, pronouns, and verbs in German.
usage: Example: Er ist nicht hier. (He is not here.)
spanish: Para negar el verbo 'sein' en alemán, se coloca 'nicht' después del verbo conjugado. 'Ich bin nicht' = Yo no soy/estoy.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after accusative prepositions like 'für' (for), the pronoun 'ich' becomes {{c1::mich}} (me) and 'du' becomes {{c2::dich}} (you).
explanation: 'für' is a preposition that always demands the accusative case.
usage: Examples: Das ist für mich. (This is for me.) | Ich habe etwas für dich. (I have something for you.)
spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones acusativas como 'für', 'ich' se convierte en 'mich' y 'du' en 'dich'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'er' (he) becomes {{c1::ihm}} (him) and 'sie' (she) becomes {{c2::ihr}} (her).
explanation: 'mit' is a preposition that always demands the dative case.
usage: Examples: Ich gehe mit ihm. (I am going with him.) | Wir sprechen mit ihr. (We are speaking with her.)
spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones dativas como 'mit', 'er' se convierte en 'ihm' y 'sie' en 'ihr'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'wir' (we) becomes {{c1::uns}} (us) and 'ihr' (you all) becomes {{c2::euch}} (you all).
explanation: Dative forms for first and second-person plural pronouns.
usage: Examples: Komm mit uns! (Come with us!) | Ich fahre mit euch. (I am driving with you all.)
spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', 'wir' se convierte en 'uns' y 'ihr' en 'euch'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'sie' (they) becomes {{c1::ihnen}} (them) and formal 'Sie' (you) becomes {{c2::Ihnen}} (you formal).
explanation: Note that 'Ihnen' (formal you) is always capitalized, whereas 'ihnen' (them) is lowercase.
usage: Examples: Wie geht es Ihnen? (How are you? - formal) | Ich arbeite mit ihnen. (I work with them.)
spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', el pronombre 'sie' (ellos/ellas) se convierte en 'ihnen' y 'Sie' (formal) se convierte en 'Ihnen'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To form a question in German using the verb 'sein', you swap the subject and verb: 'Are you ready?' is '{{c1::Bist du bereit?}}' and 'Are you sure (formal)?' is '{{c2::Sind Sie sicher?}}'.
explanation: German questions are formed by placing the inflected verb at the first position (verb-initial).
usage: Examples: Bist du bereit? (Are you ready?) | Sind Sie sicher? (Are you sure? - formal)
spanish: Para formar preguntas en alemán con 'sein', se invierte el sujeto y el verbo. 'Bist du bereit?' = ¿Estás listo?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, neuter nouns (like 'das Kind' or 'das Buch') are replaced by the personal pronoun {{c1::es}}. Example: Das Buch ist alt. {{c2::Es}} ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)
explanation: Neuter nouns are referred to by 'es', masculine by 'er', and feminine by 'sie'.
usage: Example: Das Buch ist alt. Es ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)
spanish: En alemán, los sustantivos neutros se reemplazan por el pronombre 'es'. Por ejemplo, 'das Buch' -> 'es'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the gender-neutral demonstrative phrase used to say 'this is / that is / these are / those are'? {{c1::Das ist}} or {{c1::Das sind}}.
explanation: 'Das ist' is used for singular subjects, and 'Das sind' is used for plural subjects, regardless of gender.
usage: Examples: Das ist mein Freund. (This is my friend.) | Das sind meine Bücher. (These are my books.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Esto es / Estos son' en alemán de manera invariable? Das ist (singular) / Das sind (plural).

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'Who is that?'? {{c1::Wer ist das?}}
explanation: Wer is the question word for 'Who' in German.
usage: Dialogue: - Wer ist das? - Das ist meine Schwester. (- Who is that? - That is my sister.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Quién es ese/a?' en alemán? Wer ist das?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'What is that?'? {{c1::Was ist das?}}
explanation: Was is the question word for 'What' in German.
usage: Example: Was ist das dort auf dem Tisch? (What is that there on the table?)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Qué es eso?' en alemán? Was ist das?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the singular masculine subject pronoun? {{c1::er}}
explanation: Used for male persons and any masculine grammatical noun (e.g. 'der Tisch' -> 'er ist').
usage: Example: Wo ist der Stuhl? Er ist hier. (Where is the chair? It is here.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular masculino en alemán? er

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the singular feminine subject pronoun? {{c1::sie}}
explanation: Used for female persons and any feminine grammatical noun (e.g. 'die Tasche' -> 'sie ist').
usage: Example: Wo ist die Tasche? Sie ist weg. (Where is the bag? It is gone.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular femenino en alemán? sie

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, the formal 'you' is '{{c1::Sie}}' and the informal plural 'you all' is '{{c2::ihr}}'.
explanation: The formal 'Sie' is always capitalized and conjugates like the plural 'they' (sie). The informal 'ihr' is lowercase and has its own conjugation.
usage: Formal: Sind Sie müde? | Informal plural: Seid ihr müde?
spanish: En alemán, el 'usted' formal es 'Sie' (siempre en mayúsculas) y el 'vosotros/ustedes' informal es 'ihr'.
